sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Tracheostomy and laryngectomy patients may deteriorate from tube blockage, displacement, secretion load, or ventilatory failure. Prehospital priorities are recognising that the stoma is the default airway route, oxygenating the patient, relieving simple obstruction, and escalating rapidly if ventilation is failing.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Increased work of breathing, cyanosis, falling oxygen saturation, or reduced conscious state
- Little or no airflow through the tracheostomy tube or stoma
- Gurgling, stridor, unusual upper-airway noise, wheeze, or poor chest rise
- Inability to pass a suction catheter or remove an inner cannula
- Ventilated patient with rising airway pressures or poor delivered volumes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- The default route of oxygenation and ventilation should be the stoma unless a clearly patent upper airway is known and usable
- If it is unclear whether the patient has a laryngectomy or tracheostomy, oxygen via the stoma remains the first priority and oxygen to the face can be added if resources allow
- Patients and carers often know the device and usual rescue steps; use that information where safe
- External speaking valves, heat-moisture exchangers, caps, or filters may need removal if they are contributing to obstruction

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Apply oxygen to the stoma first
- Reposition the patient and remove obvious external attachments that may be worsening obstruction if this can be done safely
- Call early for clinicians skilled in airway rescue if the patient is deteriorating
- If two oxygen sources are available and upper-airway patency is uncertain, consider oxygen to both stoma and face while assessment continues
- Prioritise urgent transport for persistent distress, poor airflow, or reduced conscious state

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Assess patency by attempting to pass a suction catheter without suction first; if secretions are present, suction the stoma or tracheostomy within training and scope
- Use ETCO2 where ventilation is being assisted
- Attempt bag-valve-mask ventilation via the stoma when spontaneous ventilation is inadequate
- Do not remove the tracheostomy tube unless it is completely obstructed and the patient cannot be ventilated through it
- If there is major air leak through the mouth during attempted stoma ventilation, reassess upper-airway patency; if ventilating via the upper airway, occlude the stoma with an airtight dressing
- Escalate early for advanced airway rescue if obstruction, displacement, or ventilatory failure persists

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Laryngectomy patients have no meaningful upper-airway route to the lungs, so stoma management is essential
- Tracheostomy patients may still have a partially patent upper airway, which changes rescue options if stoma ventilation is failing
- Document device type if known, suction catheter passage, attachments removed, ventilation route used, and response to interventions
